metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the 'git-absent-omit' test (or a companion test) asserts a DEBUG-level log record is emitted when git resolution fails, not only that the return value is version-only.
findings.finding: AC#3 requires that the resolver 'emits a debug log when git is unavailable', but the named unit test 'git-absent-omit' is not specified to assert on the log output — only on the return value. If the test only checks the return string, the debug-log requirement is uncovered by any proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#3 and unit test list 'git-absent-omit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC#3 or the unit-test list: 'git-absent-omit asserts both that the return value contains no SHA and that exactly one DEBUG log record matching "rebar version git resolution failed" (or similar) is captured (e.g. via `pytest`'s `caplog` fixture).'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new gate-code SHA resolver in `signing.py` should use `run_git` from `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py` (the codebase's established git wrapper, 10+ call-sites) rather than raw `subprocess.run`, or explicitly document why `signing.py` is intentionally exempted from the `run_git` consolidation (e.g. to keep `signing.py` a stdlib-only leaf module).
findings.finding: The plan instructs implementers to use raw `subprocess.run` (co-locating with `head_sha()` in `signing.py`) rather than the codebase-established `run_git()` wrapper in `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py`. `run_git` was introduced explicitly to consolidate 'the dozen hand-rolled `_git()` wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract' — yet the plan cargo-cults the pre-consolidation idiom and even reinforces it with 'do NOT invent a new git wrapper', treating the pre-existing raw pattern as authoritative over the consolidated one.
findings.location: What section — gate-code SHA/version resolver, signing.py; 'do NOT invent a new git wrapper'
findings.suggested_fix: Check `signing.py`'s import constraints. If it already imports from `rebar._store` or can do so, use `run_git(dir_of_rebar_file, 'rev-parse', '--short', 'HEAD', check=False)` and `run_git(dir_of_rebar_file, 'status', '--porcelain', check=False)`. If `signing.py` must remain a stdlib-only leaf (which the docstring does not state), add an explicit comment explaining the exemption so future authors don't replicate the pattern unnecessarily.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the dirty check is scoped to rebar source changes only: add a path limiter to `git status --porcelain` (e.g. `git -C <rebar_repo_root> status --porcelain -- <rebar_src_subpath>`) OR add a check that `<dir of rebar.__file__>` is the root of its own git repo (not a subdirectory of a larger repo) before running the dirty check, falling back to SHA-only when it is not.
findings.finding: When rebar is editable-installed inside the target project's git repo (the dominant developer workflow), `git -C <dir of rebar.__file__> status --porcelain` walks up to the HOST project's git root — not the rebar source repo — and reports dirty state for ANY working-tree change in the host project. This causes the `-dirty` suffix to fire on every active development session, even when rebar source itself is unchanged, making the dirty flag meaningless as a gate-code cleanliness signal.
findings.location: What section — resolver spec: `git -C <dir of rebar.__file__> status --porcelain`
findings.suggested_fix: Before running `git status --porcelain`, confirm that `git -C <dir of rebar.__file__> rev-parse --show-toplevel` returns a path that equals or is an ancestor of `<dir of rebar.__file__>` but is NOT the host project's root (i.e., rebar has its own git root distinct from the caller's repo). If they share a root, skip the dirty check (fall back to SHA only, no `-dirty`). Alternatively, limit the status scope with a path argument: `git -C <rebar_repo_root> status --porcelain -- <relative_rebar_src_path>`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the `git status --porcelain` dirty-check to the rebar source directory by adding an explicit pathspec (e.g. `git -C <rebar-pkg-dir> status --porcelain -- .`) so only changes within the rebar source tree set the `-dirty` flag, not unrelated files elsewhere in the enclosing repo.
findings.finding: `git -C <rebar-pkg-dir> status --porcelain` without a pathspec traverses up to the enclosing repo root and reports ALL dirty files in that repo, not only rebar source files. The plan specifies '-dirty iff … any … change in the rebar source tree', but the described command cannot deliver that scoping — any unrelated dirty file in the surrounding repo will trigger the flag.
findings.location: What section — resolver spec: '`git -C <dir of rebar.__file__> status --porcelain` is non-empty (any staged / modified-tracked / untracked change in the rebar source tree)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append `-- .` (or the absolute path of `rebar.__file__`'s directory) to the status command: `["git", "-C", rebar_dir, "status", "--porcelain", "--", "."]`. The `-- .` pathspec, combined with the `-C rebar_dir` CWD, limits status output to files under the rebar package directory only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'reusing the `head_sha()` idiom' means structurally mirroring the pattern (same error-handling, same subprocess approach) rather than literally calling `head_sha()` — and explicitly state that `--short` requires a separate `subprocess.run` call, not a direct reuse of the `head_sha()` return value.
findings.finding: The plan says the new resolver will reuse 'the `head_sha()` idiom' for its subprocess call, but `head_sha()` in `signing.py:344-350` runs `git … rev-parse HEAD` (full 40-char SHA) with no `--short` flag. The plan's resolver spec requires `rev-parse --short HEAD`. These are different invocations; the existing `head_sha()` cannot be called directly to get a short SHA — a new invocation is needed.
findings.location: What section: 'SHA: `git -C <dir of rebar.__file__> rev-parse --short HEAD` … reusing the `head_sha()` idiom'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the plan to say: 'Mirror the `head_sha()` pattern (same OSError guard, same `subprocess.run` structure) but issue a separate `git -C … rev-parse --short HEAD` call — do not modify or directly invoke `head_sha()` since it returns the full SHA.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `refresh_attestation()` (attest.py:446) should also carry the `rebar-version:` stamp, and whether the stamp is added inside `build_manifest` itself (affecting all callers) or threaded as a new optional parameter (requiring explicit call-site updates for each caller).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the `rebar-version:` stamp should appear in manifests produced by `refresh_attestation()` (attest.py:446–481), which calls `build_manifest` independently. If the stamp is injected inside `build_manifest` (the natural reading of 'Append one `rebar-version:` line to BOTH manifests'), drift-refreshed attestations will also carry the stamp — but the plan is silent on this. If instead the stamp is injected at call sites around `build_manifest`, `refresh_attestation` is silently omitted. This is an unspecified processing protocol co-located with the schema change.
findings.location: What / 'Append one `rebar-version:` line to BOTH manifests' + `build_manifest` (attest.py:177)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a named prefix constant (e.g. `_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX = 'rebar-version:'`) in `attest.py` and specify that both `build_manifest`/`_verdict_manifest` and `manifest_rebar_version()` reference it — mirroring the `_REGVER_PREFIX` pattern.
findings.finding: The manifest line prefix string/constant for `rebar-version:` is never defined in the plan. Every analogous field in `attest.py` uses a named module-level constant (`_REGVER_PREFIX = 'regver:'`, `_DEP_PREFIX = 'dep'`, `_DISABLED_PREFIX = 'disabled_builtins:'`), and both the writer and the parser reference that same constant. The plan says `manifest_rebar_version()` 'mirrors `manifest_regver`' but does not name the constant or the exact line prefix string, leaving an implementer to invent it — risking a mismatch between what `build_manifest`/`_verdict_manifest` emit and what `manifest_rebar_version()` matches.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'Define `_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX = "rebar-version:"` in `attest.py` (mirroring `_REGVER_PREFIX`); both emitters append `f"{_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX} {stamp}"` and `manifest_rebar_version()` matches against it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or confirm expand-contract sequencing) for the `rebar-version:` manifest line — describe how to safely strip or ignore the stamp if downstream stories are cancelled or the format must change.
findings.finding: The plan changes the output format of both `build_manifest` and `_verdict_manifest` by appending a new `rebar-version:` line, which breaks any strict line-by-line consumer (tests are explicitly noted as needing updates), but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing if the stamp must be removed or if story 2/3 deployment fails.
findings.location: What — 'Append one rebar-version: line to BOTH manifests'; AC — 'Existing tests asserting on build_manifest / _verdict_manifest output pass after being updated for the new line.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: (1) the stamp line can be removed by reverting the two append calls in `attest.py` and `transition.py`; (2) pre-stamp manifests continue to validate unchanged (already stated); (3) any consumer of `manifest_rebar_version()` receives `None` and must treat that as a no-op — document this contract explicitly so story 3 implementers know the safe degradation path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `rebar-version:` field format contract and `manifest_rebar_version()` API semantics in a durable artifact (e.g., a CLAUDE.md section, a design-doc, or an in-code module-level docstring in `attest.py`) so stories 2 and 3 have a stable reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new manifest field contract (`rebar-version:`) and a new parser API (`manifest_rebar_version`) that stories 2 (build-baking) and 3 (show/verify surfacing) will depend on, but no update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc is planned to capture this contract as a durable artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `attest.py` (or a CLAUDE.md entry) documenting: (1) the `rebar-version:` field key, (2) the value format `"<version> (<short-sha>[-dirty])" | "<version>"`, (3) the parser contract (present → stamp string, absent → None), and (4) the invariant that `compute_validity` must never read this field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all production consumers of `build_manifest` and `_verdict_manifest` output (e.g. `verify_signature`, any downstream parsers) and confirm each tolerates the added `rebar-version:` line — or explicitly note that the signing domain excludes this line.
findings.finding: The plan changes the byte output of both `build_manifest` and `_verdict_manifest` by adding a new `rebar-version:` line, but only acknowledges test callers needing updates — it does not enumerate or assess production consumers of the manifest string (e.g. `verify_signature`, any downstream parser) that may depend on the exact manifest format or byte count.
findings.location: ## What — 'Append one rebar-version: line to BOTH manifests' / ## Scope — 'show/verify_signature surfacing = story 3'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the ## What or ## Scope section: 'Confirm that `verify_signature` and any other manifest consumer ignore or tolerate unrecognised trailing lines; if the signing domain covers the full manifest, document that `verify_signature` must be updated atomically with this story or the stamped line must be appended after the signed region.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_verdict_manifest` file reference from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161` (the actual definition site — the function was split into a module-size-seam sibling off `transition.py`).
findings.finding: The plan locates `_verdict_manifest` at `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282`, but that function actually lives in the sibling module `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`. Line 282 of `transition.py` is inside a comment block describing the delegation to `close_ticket`, not the function definition. Any implementer following the plan's file reference will look in the wrong file.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan's 'What' section to read: '`_verdict_manifest` — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161` (completion-verifier)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the edit-target file for `_verdict_manifest` from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161` throughout the plan.
findings.finding: `_verdict_manifest` is defined in `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` at line 161, not in `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` as the plan states. `transition.py:282` is actually a call to `close_ticket()`. The plan names the wrong file as the edit target.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` with `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161` in the What section. The implementing diff must touch `transition_close.py`, not `transition.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the implementation target for `_verdict_manifest` from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`.
findings.finding: The plan locates `_verdict_manifest` at `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` but the function actually lives in `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`. An implementer following the plan to the wrong file will miss the edit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the file path for `_verdict_manifest` from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`.
findings.finding: `_verdict_manifest` is cited at the wrong file path. The plan says '`_verdict_manifest` — `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282`' but the function actually lives at `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`. An executor following the plan will open `transition.py`, find nothing at line 282 (which is the `_cascade_open_parent` call), and may incorrectly add the stamp to the wrong module.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
